title: The Payroll-to-Revenue Ratio Most Australian SMEs Have Never Calculated
linkedin:
The FWC wage increase is now in your payroll. The question is what it's done to your ratio.

Most service business owners track payroll as a dollar amount. What they rarely calculate is the payroll-to-revenue ratio: total employment costs as a percentage of revenue, tracked year on year.

That distinction matters now more than it usually would.

The FWC decision took effect from the first full pay period of July. For every business with award-covered staff, the numerator in that ratio moved immediately. Whether the ratio has worsened, held, or improved depends on one thing: where it was already trending before the increase landed.

Here's the pattern that comes up repeatedly when we work through this with service businesses at EOFY: the ratio has already been drifting higher for two or three years, and the July increase is the latest increment in a longer trend rather than a new event. Headcount added ahead of growth that didn't fully arrive. Rosters that expanded informally through small decisions. Utilisation that peaked and didn't recover.

A business whose payroll-to-revenue ratio has moved from 52 percent to 56 percent over three years has lost $160,000 in annual profit at $4 million in revenue. That amount doesn't appear anywhere on the P&L as a named line. It's only visible when you track the ratio across years.

The first fortnight of FY27 is when this calculation is most worth doing. The EOFY data is complete. The FY27 cost structure is brand new. And the pattern of the year ahead isn't yet set.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Quick question for July: have you calculated your payroll-to-revenue ratio from this year's EOFY data?

It's total employment costs divided by total revenue, as a percentage. Most business owners know the dollar figure but not the ratio — and the ratio is what shows whether payroll has been growing in line with revenue or quietly outpacing it over the last few years.

The FWC wage increase has just moved the cost floor up. If your ratio has been creeping higher already, this is the week to name it before the new year's pattern is set.

What's your experience been with managing payroll costs this year? #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
google:
July 1 has passed and the FWC annual wage increase is now running in your payroll. For service businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, and the east coast, this is the moment to look at a number most owners rarely calculate: the payroll-to-revenue ratio.

The payroll-to-revenue ratio takes your total employment costs for the year — wages, superannuation, payroll tax, and workers' compensation — and divides by total revenue. It shows whether payroll has been growing in line with revenue or quietly outpacing it.

With twelve months of complete EOFY data now available, this ratio is calculable for the first time in the year. And for many service businesses, it tells a different story from the dollar-cost figure alone. Headcount added ahead of growth that didn't fully arrive. Rosters that expanded informally over time. Hours that shifted from productive work toward administration without anyone deciding it.

At $4 million in revenue, four percentage points of drift in this ratio is $160,000 in annual profit that no longer reaches the bottom line — and it doesn't show up on the P&L as a named item.

The first two weeks of July are the natural window to calculate this baseline, see whether the trend has been drifting, and decide whether FY27 calls for a structural response.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
